_about: Curated etymologies for key magical terms — the podcast layer. Accurate but concise; verify/expand against OED-equivalent sources before recording. Keyed by lexicon root slug. 'origin' = language chain, 'first' = attestation note, 'note' = the story, 'hook' = a podcast angle.

wizard.first: c. 1440 as ‘philosopher, sage’; ‘one with magic power’ by the 1550s
wizard.note: A wizard was first simply a wise man; the sinister magical sense is a later hardening of an honorific.

sorcerer.origin: Old French sorcier ← Vulgar Latin *sortiarius, from Latin sors, sortem (‘lot, fate’)
sorcerer.note: A sorcerer is literally a ‘caster of lots’ — one who manipulates fate. The word hides an ancient divinatory practice inside it.

witch.origin: Old English wicce (fem.) / wicca (masc.), akin to wiccian ‘to practice magic’
witch.note: One of the oldest magic-words in English, and one of the murkiest — its pre-history is genuinely uncertain, perhaps tied to a root for ‘to bend’ or ‘sacred rite.’
witch.hook: The word older than the witch trials.

warlock.first: ‘traitor’ in OE; ‘male witch / one in league with the Devil’ by the 1500s (esp. Scots)
warlock.note: Not a male counterpart to ‘witch’ by origin at all — a warlock is an oath-breaker, a covenant-traitor.
warlock.hook: The broken-oath etymology behind every fantasy warlock.

mage.origin: Latin magus ← Greek magos ← Old Persian maguš
mage.note: The magi were the Zoroastrian priestly caste of Persia. Their name is the root of magic, magician, and the biblical ‘wise men.’
mage.hook: From a Persian priesthood to a fantasy class.

magus.origin: Latin magus ← Greek magos ← Old Persian maguš
magus.note: Same root as mage; ‘magus’ keeps the loftier, hieratic flavor of the Persian priest-astrologer.
magus.hook: The three magi were astrologers, not kings.

shaman.note: A late loan into English from Siberian religion; sometimes further linked to Sanskrit śramaṇa (‘ascetic’), though that’s contested.
shaman.hook: The one clearly non-European word in the magic lexicon.

druid.note: *dru- (‘oak’ / ‘strong’) + *weid- (‘to see, to know’). The same *weid- gives us wit, vision, and Sanskrit Veda.

cleric.origin: Late Latin clericus ← Greek klērikos, from klēros ‘a lot, inheritance’
cleric.note: The clergy are ‘God’s lot’ (klēros) — the same fate/lot metaphor that hides inside ‘sorcerer,’ pointed the opposite, holy direction.
cleric.hook: Cleric vs sorcerer: two casters of the same ‘lot.’

necromancer.note: Originally divination BY the dead, not raising them. Medieval Latin folk-etymologised it to nigromantia — ‘black arts’ — swapping death for the color black.
necromancer.hook: How ‘death-divination’ became ‘the black arts’ by a spelling mistake.

conjure.origin: Latin coniurare ‘to swear together’ (con- + iurare ‘to swear’)
conjure.note: To conjure a spirit was to bind it by a shared oath — a conspiracy with the supernatural. Same root as ‘jury’ and ‘conspire.’

hex.origin: German Hexe ‘witch’ ← Old High German hagazussa ‘hedge-rider’
hex.note: A young loan-word for a very old idea — the hagazussa ‘rode the hedge’ between the tame village and the wild beyond.

curse.origin: Old English curs — origin genuinely unknown
curse.note: One of the rare English words with no established cognate in any related language; the etymology is a real dead-end, which is itself eerie.
curse.hook: A curse-word whose own origins are cursed to obscurity.

enchant.origin: Old French enchanter ← Latin incantare ‘to sing a spell upon’ (in- + cantare ‘to sing’)
enchant.note: To enchant is literally to sing over someone. The same cantare gives chant, incantation, and cantor.
enchant.hook: Magic you sing: the song in ‘enchantment.’

scry.origin: Aphetic form of descry ← Old French descrier ‘to cry out, announce’
scry.note: Scrying literally means ‘catching sight of’ — a shortened ‘descry.’ MTG turned this quiet divination word into one of its signature keywords.
scry.hook: From ‘to spy out’ to a card mechanic.

divine.origin: Latin divinare ‘to foresee’, from divinus ‘of a god’
divine.note: To divine is to do what the gods do — to know the future. Same root as ‘deity’ and ‘divinity.’

augur.origin: Latin augur, a Roman priest who read bird-signs; perhaps from augere ‘to increase, bless’
augur.note: The augurs read the flight of birds to bless state actions; ‘inauguration’ still carries the ritual.

oracle.origin: Latin oraculum, from orare ‘to speak, pray’
oracle.note: An oracle is a ‘speaking-place’ or the speech itself — the god’s mouth. Same root as oration and orator.

prophesy.origin: Greek prophētēs ‘one who speaks before/for’ (pro- + phēnai ‘to speak’)
prophesy.note: A prophet speaks FOR a god (and thus, often, before the event). The spelling split prophecy (noun) / prophesy (verb) is a later English tidy-up.
prophesy.hook: Prophecy is about speaking, not (only) foreseeing.

sibyl.note: The Sibyls were antiquity’s freelance prophetesses; the Cumaean Sibyl guided Aeneas to the underworld.
sibyl.hook: The women who out-ranked the oracles.

haruspex.note: Etruscan-derived Roman diviners who read the liver of sacrificed animals against a bronze map of the sky.
haruspex.hook: Reading the future in a sheep’s liver.

alchemy.origin: Medieval Latin alkimia ← Arabic al-kīmiyāʾ (al- ‘the’ + kīmiyā)
alchemy.note: The Arabic preserves a Greek word — perhaps khēmia (‘the Egyptian art,’ from Khem, ‘the black land’) or khymeia (‘pouring, infusion’). The al- is the Arabic article, as in algebra, alkali, alcohol.
alchemy.hook: The Arabic ‘al-’ that smuggled a science into Europe.

elixir.origin: Medieval Latin ← Arabic al-iksīr ‘the philosopher’s stone’ ← Greek xērion ‘drying powder’
elixir.note: Began as a dry medicinal powder for wounds, became the transmuting ‘stone,’ then the draught of eternal life.
elixir.hook: How wound-powder became the water of immortality.

quintessence.origin: Medieval Latin quinta essentia ‘the fifth essence’
quintessence.note: Aristotle’s incorruptible fifth element (after earth, water, air, fire); the alchemists sought to distil it out of matter.

transmute.note: The core verb of the whole alchemical project — to move a substance across the boundary of its own nature.

azoth.origin: Arabic al-zāʾūq ‘the mercury’, cross-bred with a mystical alphabet-word
azoth.note: Said to be built from the first and last letters of the Latin, Greek and Hebrew alphabets (A + Z/Ω/Tav) — ‘the beginning and the end’ — while also meaning quicksilver. A word designed to be a symbol.
azoth.hook: A word engineered to mean ‘alpha and omega.’

hermetic.origin: From Hermes Trismegistus, legendary sage of the alchemical texts
hermetic.note: The airtight ‘hermetic seal’ is named for the alchemists’ god of the sealed vessel — hidden knowledge kept literally airtight.
hermetic.hook: Why your jam jar is ‘hermetically’ sealed.

talisman.origin: Arabic ṭilsam ← Byzantine Greek telesma ‘consecration’, from telein ‘to complete/consecrate’
talisman.note: A talisman is a ‘completed’ or consecrated object — made powerful by ritual, not found powerful. Contrast the amulet, which merely protects.
talisman.hook: Made, not found: talisman vs amulet.

amulet.origin: Latin amuletum ‘a charm’; perhaps from amoliri ‘to avert, remove’
amulet.note: Where a talisman is actively charged, an amulet passively wards — its very name may mean ‘the thing that carries trouble away.’

sigil.origin: Latin sigillum ‘little sign/seal’, diminutive of signum ‘sign’
sigil.note: A sigil is a ‘little seal’ — a sign compressed into a mark of power. Same root as ‘signal’ and ‘signature.’
sigil.hook: How a signature becomes a spell.

rune.origin: Old English rūn ‘secret, mystery, whisper’ ← Proto-Germanic *rūnō
rune.note: The runic letters were named for secrecy itself — to know the runes was to know hidden things. The Finnish loan ‘runo’ means ‘poem, song.’

grimoire.origin: French grimoire, an alteration of grammaire ‘grammar’
grimoire.note: A grimoire is, etymologically, just a ‘grammar’ — a book of learning. When most learning was Latin and mysterious, the grammar-book became the spell-book.
grimoire.hook: Grammar and grimoire are the same word.

glamour.note: Astonishingly, glamour is ALSO a mutation of ‘grammar’ — a second magic-word born from the mystique of book-learning. Fashion-glamour is a 20c. softening of ‘a magic illusion.’

tome.origin: Latin tomus ← Greek tomos ‘a slice, a roll of papyrus’, from temnein ‘to cut’
tome.note: A tome is literally a ‘cut’ — one section sliced from a longer work. The weighty modern sense is a later inflation.
tome.hook: Every ‘tome’ was once just one volume of many.

codex.origin: Latin codex / caudex ‘tree-trunk, block of wood’
codex.note: Before paper, the codex was a stack of wax-covered wooden tablets bound at one edge — the direct ancestor of the page-turning book, versus the rolled scroll.
codex.hook: The book began as a woodpile.

scroll.origin: Middle English scrowle, alteration of scrowe ‘scrap’, influenced by rolle ‘roll’
scroll.note: The scroll’s name records the losing side of a media war — the format the codex replaced, now revived only in the verb we do to phones.
scroll.hook: We ‘scroll’ our phones with a dead book-format’s name.

phylactery.origin: Greek phylaktērion ‘a safeguard, amulet’, from phylassein ‘to guard’
phylactery.note: First the Jewish tefillin, then any protective amulet or scripture-case. Modern fantasy (D&D) reassigned it to a lich’s soul-jar — a total shift of referent while keeping the ‘container of power’ core.
phylactery.hook: The amulet that fantasy turned into a soul-jar.

pentacle.origin: Medieval Latin pentaculum, ult. Greek pente ‘five’
pentacle.note: A five-figured talismanic disc (the ‘pentagram’ is strictly the drawn star; the pentacle is the whole charged object). Renaissance magicians wore them for protection during conjuration.
pentacle.hook: Pentacle vs pentagram: object vs figure.

arcane.origin: Latin arcanus ‘secret, shut up’, from arca ‘a chest, box’
arcane.note: The arcane is what’s locked in the box (arca) — the same root as ‘ark.’ MTG made ‘Arcane’ a literal spell-type.
arcane.hook: The secret in the box: arca → arcane → ark.

occult.origin: Latin occultus ‘hidden, concealed’, pp. of occulere ‘to cover over’
occult.note: Astronomers still say a moon ‘occults’ a star — passes in front and hides it. The occult is simply ‘the hidden,’ before it was ‘the sinister.’

mystic.origin: Greek mystikos, from mystēs ‘an initiate’, from myein ‘to shut (the eyes/lips)’
mystic.note: A mystic is one who has ‘shut their eyes’ — closed to the outer world, sworn to secrecy at the mysteries. Same root as ‘mystery’ and ‘mute.’

geas.origin: Irish geis ‘a taboo, a solemn injunction’
geas.note: In Irish myth a geas is a binding prohibition laid on a hero — break it and doom follows. Fantasy borrowed it as a compulsion spell.
geas.hook: The Celtic taboo that became a spell.

theurgy.note: The Neoplatonists’ high, sacramental magic — working WITH the gods to ascend — as opposed to goēteia, the low magic of coercion.
theurgy.hook: High magic vs low magic, in two Greek words.

goetia.origin: Greek goēteia ‘sorcery’, from goēs ‘a sorcerer who howls charms’
goetia.note: The ‘howling’ root marks goetia as ritual noise-magic — the coercive spirit-binding later catalogued in the Lesser Key of Solomon.

cauldron.origin: Old North French caudron ← Latin caldaria ‘hot bath’, from calidus ‘hot’
cauldron.note: Just a big hot pot by origin — the witch’s cauldron is a domestic object made sinister by association.
cauldron.hook: The kitchen pot that became a witch’s icon.

besom.origin: Old English besma ‘bundle of twigs, broom’
besom.note: The witch’s broom is, in the oldest English, simply the ‘besom’ — the everyday twig-broom, later the flying steed of the sabbat.

grail.origin: Old French graal ‘cup, dish’ ← Medieval Latin gradalis
grail.note: Chrétien de Troyes’ ‘graal’ was just a serving-dish; only later did it become the Holy Grail. The word’s own sanctity was retro-fitted.
grail.hook: The Holy Grail started as a dinner plate.

enchanter.origin: Latin incantare 'to sing a spell on' (via Old French enchanteur)
enchanter.note: The agent-noun of enchant; the -ess form (enchantress) feminised it. A singer of spells.

conjurer.note: One who binds spirits by oath — and, by the 1500s, the street-performer who only seems to. Both senses survive.
conjurer.hook: When 'conjurer' split into sorcerer and street-magician.

hierophant.origin: Greek hierophantēs 'revealer of holy things' (hieros 'sacred' + phainein 'to show')
hierophant.note: The chief priest of the Eleusinian Mysteries, who displayed the sacred objects. Now also a Tarot trump.
hierophant.hook: The priest who ran antiquity's secret rites.

thaumaturge.note: Applied first to miracle-working saints, then to conjurers. Same ergon ('work') that hides in theurgy and energy.

occultist.note: A Victorian coinage for a practitioner of the 'occult sciences' — the word arrives exactly when Spiritualism does.
occultist.hook: A job title invented by the Victorians.

exorcist.origin: Greek exorkistēs, from exorkizein 'to bind by oath' (ex- + horkos 'oath')
exorcist.first: late 14c.; once a minor clerical order
exorcist.note: You don't expel a demon by force but by a stronger oath — the same oath-logic as conjure and abjure.

bless.origin: Old English blēdsian 'to consecrate with blood', from blōd 'blood'
bless.note: Astonishingly, to bless once meant to sprinkle with sacrificial blood; Christianity redirected its sense toward Latin benedicere ('speak well of').

spell.note: 'Gospel' is gōd-spell ('good news'); spelling out letters is the same word (a 'reading-out'). The magic sense is the youngest of the three.
spell.hook: Gospel, spelling, and spellcasting are one word.

summon.origin: Latin summonēre 'to remind privately, hint' (sub- + monēre 'to warn')
summon.note: A legal summons and a demonic summoning share a root meaning 'a formal calling-forth'. Same monēre as premonition and monitor.
summon.hook: A court summons and a demon summons, one word.

charm.note: A charm is literally a song; a 'charming' person is faintly, etymologically, casting one. The same carmen as in Carmina Burana.
charm.hook: Charm is a song that binds you.

haunt.origin: Old French hanter 'to frequent, resort to'
haunt.note: To haunt first meant merely 'to hang around a place'. Ghosts haunt because they keep frequenting; the spooky sense is a late English gift.
haunt.hook: Ghosts don't haunt — they just won't leave.

possess.origin: Latin possidēre 'to hold, occupy' (potis 'able' + sedēre 'to sit')
possess.note: Demonic possession is a spirit 'sitting in' the body — the same root that gives us 'property' and 'possession'.
possess.hook: The property law inside demonic possession.

ward.note: Guard is the same word borrowed back through French — a Germanic/Romance doublet. Warden, wardrobe, and toward all share it; now an MTG keyword too.
ward.hook: 'Guard' and 'ward' are the same word, split by the Channel.

chant.note: The sung root under half the vocabulary of magic — enchant, incant, cantor, and charm's cousin all trace to Latin song.

banish.origin: Old French banir, from Frankish *bannjan 'to proclaim, summon by ban'
banish.note: Same *bann as 'ban' and the marriage 'banns' — banishment was a public proclamation of expulsion, a shout as much as an act.

commune.note: To commune with spirits is to hold something in common with them; the same root as communion and community.
commune.hook: A séance is a kind of sharing.

invoke.origin: Latin invocare 'to call upon' (in- + vocare 'to call')
invoke.note: The exact opposite of evoke: to invoke is to call a power INTO oneself, to evoke is to call it OUT. Ceremonial magic keeps the distinction precise.
invoke.hook: Invoke vs evoke: calling in against calling out.

consecrate.note: The sacer root runs holy AND cursed: 'sacred', 'sacrifice', but also 'execrate'. The Romans knew the sacred was dangerous.
consecrate.hook: The sacred root that also means accursed.

incant.origin: Latin incantare 'to sing a spell on' — the learned doublet of enchant
incant.note: 'Incantation' and 'enchantment' are the SAME Latin word: one borrowed early and worn smooth through French, one borrowed late and bookish.
incant.hook: Enchant and incant: one word, borrowed twice.

bewitch.origin: Old English be- (intensive) + wiccian 'to work magic'
bewitch.note: To 'witch', intensified. The fashion sense — 'to bewitch a room' — is a softened spell; now also an MTG keyword.

exorcism.origin: Greek exorkismos, from exorkizein 'to bind by oath'
exorcism.note: The rite names its method: not force but a superior oath sworn against the spirit.
exorcism.hook: Winning a swearing-contest with a demon.

evoke.origin: Latin evocare 'to call out' (e- + vocare 'to call')
evoke.note: The Roman ritual of evocatio literally called an enemy city's gods OUT to defect before a siege — state-sponsored divine poaching.
evoke.hook: The Roman spell that stole other cities' gods.

abjure.origin: Latin abiurare 'to deny on oath' (ab- + iurare 'to swear')
abjure.note: The anti-conjuration: where you conjure by swearing an oath, you abjure by un-swearing one. Magic runs on oaths, made and forsworn.

seer.note: The plainest word in the whole divination family — just 'one who sees' — standing among a crowd of borrowed Greco-Latin -mancers.
seer.hook: The homegrown English prophet among the Greek imports.

omen.origin: Latin omen 'a foreboding sign'; ultimate root obscure
omen.note: 'Abominable' was long (wrongly) parsed as ab-omen, 'away from a good omen' — a folk-etymology so strong it changed the spelling.
omen.hook: Why 'abominable' seems to hide an omen.

pyromancy.note: One of dozens the -mancy suffix generates. Fire, being alive and unpredictable, was among the oldest oracles.
pyromancy.hook: The '-mancy' machine: a suffix that mass-produces prophets.

portent.origin: Latin portentum, from portendere 'to foretell' (por- + tendere 'to stretch')
portent.note: A portent 'stretches forward' — the future reaching back to touch the present.
portent.hook: An omen that reaches out of the future.

diviner.note: To divine is to do the gods' own work — know what is hidden. Water-diviners kept the word literal into modern times.

soothsayer.note: A soothsayer is a 'truth-sayer'. 'Sooth' has vanished from English except here and in 'forsooth'.
soothsayer.hook: The last hiding place of the word 'sooth'.

augury.note: The state art of reading omens in bird-flight; every Roman public act opened with it, and 'inauguration' still does.

geomancy.note: Divination by dots and earth-figures; it entered Europe from the Arabic 'ilm al-raml, the 'science of sand'.
geomancy.hook: Dots in the sand that grew into a whole art.

hydromancy.note: Reading the future in water — the bowl of water behind the very idea of scrying.
hydromancy.hook: The oracle in a bowl of water.

clairvoyance.note: A young word, coined by Mesmerism and Spiritualism — the newest arrival in an ancient family of seeing-words.

auspice.note: To do a thing 'under good auspices' is to recall the Roman bird-diviner (auspex) who checked whether the gods approved.
auspice.hook: Why we still act 'under good auspices'.

astrology.note: Astrology and astronomy were a single science until the 17th century pried them apart; Kepler cast horoscopes for a living.
astrology.hook: When astrology and astronomy filed for divorce.

oneiromancy.note: Dream-divination, systematised in Artemidorus' 2nd-c. Oneirocritica — antiquity's dream dictionary.

prescience.note: Foreknowledge as a kind of 'fore-science' — the same scire that gives us 'science' itself.

presage.origin: Latin praesagium 'a foreboding' (prae- + sagire 'to perceive keenly')
presage.note: The keen nose for what's coming; the sagire root also yields 'sagacious'.
presage.hook: A sharp nose for the future.

premonition.note: The warn-root monēre also builds summon, monitor, admonish — magic and administration share their vocabulary of warning.

prognostic.note: Straight out of Hippocratic medicine (the Prognostics), it drifted into weather-almanacs and fortune-telling.
prognostic.hook: From the doctor's bedside to the fortune-teller's almanac.

potion.note: 'Poison' is the very same Latin word arriving by a different French road — a potion and a poison are perfect etymological twins.
potion.hook: Potion and poison: one Latin word, two fates.

crucible.note: An ordeal is a 'crucible' by metaphor from the alchemist's fierce little pot; medieval scribes linked it to crux, 'cross'.
crucible.hook: Why a hard test is a 'crucible'.

amalgam.origin: Medieval Latin amalgama, prob. from Arabic al-malgham 'emollient, soft mass'
amalgam.note: A mercury alloy — and another Arabic al- word smuggled into science. 'Amalgamation' of ideas is a 17th-c. metaphor.
amalgam.hook: One more Arabic 'al-' hiding in plain sight.

distill.origin: Latin destillare 'to drip down' (de- + stillare 'to drip', stilla 'a drop')
distill.note: The whole apparatus is named for dripping; 'still' (the device) is just a clipping of 'distill'.

putrefy.origin: Latin putrefacere 'to make rotten' (puter 'rotten' + facere 'to make')
putrefy.note: The alchemical nigredo, the black rotting stage that had to precede perfection — rot as a rung on the ladder up.
putrefy.hook: When rotting was a step toward gold.

retort.note: The vessel is named for its bent-back neck; the 'sharp reply' retort is the same word (an answer twisted back at you).
retort.hook: The bent-neck flask and the bent-back reply.

sublimate.note: Solid straight to vapour, skipping liquid — a rising. Freud borrowed the word for desire redirected upward.
sublimate.hook: From the alembic to the analyst's couch.

tincture.note: A tincture colors a thing AND imparts its virtue; heraldry's 'tinctures' (the colors of a shield) are the same word.
tincture.hook: Color as the carrier of essence.

vitriol.origin: Medieval Latin vitriolum 'sulphate', from Latin vitrum 'glass' (for its glassy crystals)
vitriol.note: The alchemists' motto V.I.T.R.I.O.L. spelled a whole instruction; 'vitriolic' speech still carries the acid.

infernal.note: Hell is, etymologically, just 'the lower place'; Dante's Inferno fixed the fire on top of the geography.

diabolic.note: The Devil is, by his own name, 'the one who throws accusations'; 'devil' is just diabolos worn down.
diabolic.hook: The Devil is, literally, the prosecutor.

eldritch.origin: Prob. Old English el- 'foreign, other' + rīce 'realm' — 'of an otherworldly kingdom'
eldritch.note: A Scots survival Lovecraft launched into cosmic horror. It means, more or less, 'from the other kingdom'.

esoteric.note: Aristotle's 'inner' teachings, kept for initiates, against the 'exoteric' lectures for the public. The word for the members-only.
esoteric.hook: The word for the inner circle.

chthonic.note: Greek religion split its gods: the chthonic earth-and-underworld powers (Hades, Demeter) against the bright Olympians above.

wand.origin: Old Norse vöndr 'a supple rod, switch' (Proto-Germanic *wanduz, root 'to wind')
wand.note: The magician's wand is, by origin, a whippy twig you could bend. Latin's rival word virga gives us 'verge'.
wand.hook: The magic wand was just a bendy stick.

reliquary.note: A jewelled box for holy leftovers; the same root as 'relic' and 'relinquish'.
reliquary.hook: A gold box for sacred leftovers.

chalice.note: A doublet of 'calyx', a flower's cup; the Grail is the chalice raised to legend.
chalice.hook: Cup, calyx, chalice, Grail — one shape, four words.

poppet.origin: Old French poupette, from Latin puppa 'doll, girl'
poppet.note: The witch's effigy is a 'little doll'; 'puppet' is the very same word, and 'poppet' survives as a term of endearment.
poppet.hook: The witch's doll and the pet name are one word.

censer.origin: Old French encensier, from Latin incensum 'that which is burnt'
censer.note: Same incensum as 'incense' — and as being 'incensed' (inflamed) with anger.
censer.hook: Incense and being incensed: same fire.

fetish.origin: Portuguese feitiço 'charm', from Latin facticius 'made by art' (facere 'to make')
fetish.note: Portuguese sailors coined it for West African power-objects; anthropology, Marx, and Freud each borrowed it in turn.
fetish.hook: A colonial word for 'made power' that psychology stole.

crystal-ball.note: The Greeks believed clear quartz was ice frozen so hard it could never melt — so the scryer gazes into 'a ball of eternal ice'.
crystal-ball.hook: The Greeks thought crystal was permanently frozen ice.

testament.origin: Latin testamentum 'a will', from testis 'a witness'
testament.note: The Old and New 'Testaments' are 'witnessed covenants'. Same testis as testify — and, by an old Roman oath-custom, testicle.
testament.hook: Why the Bible is named after a legal will.

folio.note: A book measured in leaves; 'foliage', 'portfolio' (leaf-carrier) and Shakespeare's First Folio all grow from it.
folio.hook: Books once counted in leaves, like trees.

bestiary.note: Books where every animal is a moral lesson — the pelican as Christ, the beaver as chastity. Zoology as sermon.

manuscript.origin: Latin manu scriptus 'written by hand' (manus 'hand' + scribere 'to write')
manuscript.note: Defined entirely by hand-writing, in pointed contrast to print; abbreviated 'MS', plural 'MSS'.
manuscript.hook: A whole word meaning just 'by hand'.

herbal.note: A book of plants and their virtues — where botany, medicine, and magic were bound in one volume.
herbal.hook: The book where medicine and magic were the same subject.

treatise.origin: Old French traitis, from Latin tractare 'to handle' (trahere 'to drag')
treatise.note: A 'handling' of a subject; the same tractare as 'treat', 'tract', and 'traction' — an argument dragged out in full.
treatise.hook: To 'treat' a topic is to drag it out.

book-of-hours.origin: English rendering of Latin horae 'hours' (ult. Greek hōra 'season')
book-of-hours.note: The runaway bestseller of the Middle Ages: prayers timed to the canonical hours, and the canvas for its greatest illumination.
